_format_scope: composition + arrangement + performance + stylistic_figures. Excludes mixing/mastering/recording — user handles those via Mix Analyzer on the produced track.

_review_corrections.reason: Medication is on Lullabies to Paralyze (2005), not Songs for the Deaf (2002). Lanegan-led short track on Lullabies, not on the SFTD track listing.
Lullabies to Paralyze release year — corrects the album misattribution.

_review_corrections.before: Drums (Dave Grohl as guest drummer — kick, snare, hi-hat, toms, cymbals)
_review_corrections.after: Drums (Joey Castillo — post-Grohl-guest-era documented QOTSA drummer for Lullabies to Paralyze)
_review_corrections.reason: Lullabies to Paralyze drummer is Joey Castillo, not Grohl. Grohl was guest drummer only on Songs for the Deaf.

_reviewer_notes: ⚠️ MAJOR CORRECTION: Draft misattributed Medication to Songs for the Deaf (2002); track is actually on Lullabies to Paralyze (2005) — corrected album/year/primary drummer attribution. CASCADING IMPACT: The body text contains additional Songs-for-the-Deaf-era framings that the user may want to revise: (1) drum_style.performer + body references to 'Grohl' should read 'Joey Castillo'; (2) the special_effects 'vocal_democracy_across_album_three_lead_vocalists' framing — references to Homme/Oliveri/Lanegan as Songs for the Deaf vocalists — applies less cleanly to Lullabies to Paralyze where Oliveri had been removed from the band and vocal democracy was reduced; (3) references to 'A Song for the Dead drumming' and 'First It Giveth' as same-album peers are also incorrect. The compositional analysis (compressed economy, Lanegan whiskey-baritone identity, abrupt ending, restrained drumming) is otherwise accurate and applies to the actual track. Tuning and tempo plausible.
_high_confidence_fields: stylistic_figures around the brevity-as-statement and Lanegan vocal placement
_to_verify_fields: exact key area F# minor vs G# minor
exact bar count of intro, verses, choruses
Whether Homme provides backing vocal harmony
time_signature: 4/4 throughout. No metric trickery — the compositional choice is in BREVITY and VOCAL IDENTITY, not in meter.
key_area: F# minor area / G# minor area. Tonal center sits in a Homme-and-Lanegan-baritone-friendly minor zone with chromatic riff motion. Defensible reading: minor pentatonic + chromatic neighbor tones, no functional V-I cadence required.
tuning_documented: Drop D (D-A-D-G-B-E low to high). Standard QOTSA Songs-for-the-Deaf-era tuning.

composition.structural_blueprint: brief_riff_intro (a few bars of full-band riff statement establish key and groove — barely time to settle) -> verse (Lanegan whiskey-baritone vocal enters, riff continues, dense and purposeful) -> chorus (hook expansion, possible Homme backing harmony, vocal projects) -> brief_bridge_or_riff_return -> final_chorus -> abrupt_ending. Total ~1:55. Every section delivered at maximum compression — there is NO fat, no extended development, no second-half jam. The compositional ambition is COMPRESSED ECONOMY.

section_count_and_lengths.intro: Approximately 4-8 bars at ~130 BPM — minimal time to settle
section_count_and_lengths.bridge_or_riff_return: Brief — possibly a single 4-bar instrumental gesture between verses or before final chorus
section_count_and_lengths.outro: Effectively absent — track ends abruptly with final chorus or one-bar tag
section_count_and_lengths._note: Bar counts approximate. Track is so short that traditional verse/pre-chorus/chorus/bridge segmentation is COMPRESSED — closer to verse / chorus / verse / chorus / end with minimal connective tissue.

composition.harmonic_motion: Riff-based minor-modal with chromatic motion. Same modal center throughout. Chord changes serve the riff cycle rather than imposing functional progression. No key changes, no modulations.
composition.modal_choice: F# minor or G# minor area, Aeolian with chromatic neighbor tones. Lanegan's vocal range likely determines exact key center (he sits comfortably in low baritone — chosen key allows him to project at chest voice without straining).
composition.melodic_contour: Lanegan's verse vocal sits in a narrow baritone register with slight downward gravity at phrase ends — characteristic of his Screaming Trees and solo material. Chorus opens out slightly with longer-held notes but stays modest in range. Vocal is sung not screamed, with whiskey-grit timbre. The riff itself is a chromatic-tinged minor figure that supports rather than challenges the vocal.
composition.characteristic_riff_construction: Drop-D minor riff with chromatic motion built to fit the compressed format. Each bar delivers material — no hypnotic-repetition luxury (that's First It Giveth's territory). The riff cycles fast enough to feel constant but is short enough that the entire song completes before the listener can settle into a groove zone. Compositional discipline at the riff level.
composition.harmonic_pacing: Rapid at the bar level (every bar contributes), slow at the chord level (same modal center). Pacing is generated by the QUICK SECTION TURNOVER rather than by harmonic motion — verse to chorus to verse to chorus all happens within 90 seconds.
composition.phrase_symmetry: Largely symmetric 4-bar / 8-bar phrasing. The asymmetry is at the SONG-LEVEL — total duration is asymmetric to listener expectations of what a rock song lasts. 1:55 itself is the asymmetric statement.

instrumentation_complete: Electric guitar drop-D (Josh Homme, tube amp with moderate distortion)
Possible double-tracked rhythm guitar for stereo width
Electric bass drop-D (Nick Oliveri, pick attack, tight kick-lock)
Drum kit (Joey Castillo — post-Grohl-guest-era documented QOTSA drummer for Lullabies to Paralyze)
Possible secondary vocal harmony (Josh Homme backing)

section_instrumentation.intro: Full band riff statement — drums + guitar + bass at full density
section_instrumentation.verse: Full band continues, Lanegan enters whiskey-baritone, layering minimal
section_instrumentation.chorus: Slight expansion — possible secondary vocal harmony, fuller chord voicings, drum slightly more intense
section_instrumentation.outro: Abrupt ending — no fade, no extended coda. Final chord cuts.

arrangement.vocal_layering_strategy: Lanegan single-tracked lead foregrounded. Possible Homme backing harmony in chorus for hook emphasis but minimal layering elsewhere. Compression aesthetic doesn't allow extended vocal stacking — Lanegan's whiskey-baritone identity must come through clearly without obstruction.
arrangement.dynamic_arc_overall: Near-flat with slight chorus lift. Track is so short that there is no extended build — it operates at sustained mid-high intensity throughout, with chorus marginally fuller than verse. The compositional impact comes from the IDENTITY of the vocal performer + the BREVITY of the package, not from dynamic arc.

instrumental_role_assignment.guitar_role: Riff engine throughout. Same role in verse and chorus with slight chord-voicing broadening for chorus.
instrumental_role_assignment.bass_role: Tight kick-lock + low-end foundation. Pick attack for rhythmic clarity.
instrumental_role_assignment.drums_role: Compressed-economy backbone. Grohl restrains technical display — every hit purposeful, no extended fills, transitions accomplished with minimal cymbal accents rather than extended rolls. RESTRAINED PRECISION rather than featured spotlight (the OPPOSITE of A Song for the Dead).
instrumental_role_assignment.vocal_role: Lanegan whiskey-baritone is the IDENTITY of the track — listeners hear the song as 'the Lanegan-led one'. Vocal delivers the compressed lyric content within the 1:55 frame without melismatic indulgence.

harmonic_density_per_section.chorus: High — full band + lead vocal + possible backing harmony
harmonic_density_per_section._note: Density variation is small. Compression aesthetic doesn't allow major thinning.

arrangement_anti_patterns_avoided: No fadeout (abrupt ending preserves compression statement)
No drum feature spotlight (the opposite of A Song for the Dead — restrained drumming serves compression)

performance.tempo_feel_description: Mid-tempo compressed-economy groove at ~130 BPM. Feel is TIGHT and PURPOSEFUL — every measure delivers material. No relaxation zones. Performance must commit fully because there is no room for warming up; the track ends before any conventional song would have reached its bridge.

drum_style.feel: Restrained compressed precision — Grohl plays for the song's brevity rather than for technical display. The contrast with his A Song for the Dead drumming is part of the album's range.
techniques_documented: Kick + snare backbone driving 4/4 at ~130 BPM
Minimal fills — compression aesthetic doesn't allow room for technical display
Cymbal accents articulate brief transitions between verse and chorus
Section transitions accomplished with quick crash rather than extended rolls
drum_style.groove_feel_alignment: Mechanical-tight + groove-conscious. Drumming serves the riff and the vocal rather than competing for attention.
drum_style.fills_and_transitions: Minimal. Brief crash on chorus downbeat, brief snare accent on verse return. No extended fills.

feel_per_section.intro_riff: Riff statement, drop-D, moderate distortion, locked to drum grid
feel_per_section.verse: Continuous riff cycle supporting Lanegan vocal, picking precision
feel_per_section.chorus: Slight chord-voicing broadening, possible double-tracked rhythm widening stereo image
feel_per_section.bridge_solo: N/A — no solo, brief riff variation if present

techniques_documented: Tube amps with moderate distortion (not maximum saturation — compression context favors clarity)
Picking precision required for compressed-tempo execution
Less micro-variation than First It Giveth (compression aesthetic doesn't allow same hypnotic-repetition luxury)
guitar_style.playing_style_versus_other_grunge: Robot-rock vocabulary applied to extreme-compression context. Compared to grunge contemporaries the playing is rhythm-locked and economic. No solo, no extended jam — the discipline is unusual for 2002 rock.

vocal_delivery.performer: Mark Lanegan (lead vocal, his lead-vocal QOTSA contribution beyond harmony role on First It Giveth)
vocal_delivery.verse_delivery: Whiskey-baritone — Lanegan's documented Screaming Trees + solo material vocal lineage. Sustained chest-voice phrases with grit and slight rasp. Phrasing slightly behind-the-beat for human feel.
vocal_delivery.chorus_delivery: Sustained whiskey-baritone projection, slightly louder, possible Homme backing harmony layered subtly. Chorus is where the hook crystallizes.
vocal_delivery.outro_delivery: Track ends abruptly — vocal cuts with the band. No fadeout, no held-note tag.
documented_vocal_quirks: Lanegan's WHISKEY-BARITONE distinct from Homme's MELODIC BARITONE — different tonal areas within same vocal range category
Multiple complete takes selected/comped per Eric Valentine's documented production approach
Less vocal layering than First It Giveth — compression aesthetic favors single-tracked clarity
Lanegan's phrasing carries the song's emotional gravity; the brevity makes each phrase weightier

performance_anti_patterns_avoided: No technical drumming display (compression aesthetic priority)
No vocal melisma (Lanegan delivers straight, the brevity demands directness)

stylistic_figures._note: Production gestures and figures of style — the rises, drops, transitions, climaxes, special effects that define the track's compositional identity beyond pure pitch/rhythm content.

transitions_between_sections.description: Lanegan's voice simply enters on top of the riff cycle — no fill, no riser. The whiskey-baritone arrives as a tonal event rather than a structural event.
Single cymbal crash on the chorus downbeat marks the transition. No extended fill, no riser. Compression aesthetic forbids elaborate transitions.
Direct return to verse density without breakdown. The track does not allow itself the luxury of a breakdown bar.
Track ENDS rather than fades. Final chord cuts; sometimes a brief crash. The brevity is sealed by the abrupt ending — no extended outro, no fadeout, no held-note tag.

risers_and_builds.where: N/A — no risers in this track
risers_and_builds.description: Compression aesthetic does not allow rising gestures. Track operates at sustained mid-high intensity from intro to end. No build, no riser samples, no extended dynamic ramps.

cutoffs_and_filter_moves.description: Compression aesthetic favors clarity over filter automation. Track does not feature audible filter sweeps or dynamic spectral shifts.

drops_and_breakdowns.description: Track has no breakdown sections. Density variation between verse and chorus is small; no point in the track strips back to bare elements.

climax_moments.description: The final chorus is the densest moment — possibly with extra Homme backing vocal, slightly fuller drum activity. Climax is at the END of the track, immediately followed by the abrupt termination.

special_effects_and_textural_devices.description: Track at 1:55 within an album that includes longer tracks. Brevity is the compositional statement. Listeners experience the song as a STATEMENT rather than as an extended journey.

Mark Lanegan as lead vocalist (not just harmony) gives the track distinct vocal IDENTITY within the album's vocal democracy. Listeners hear it as 'the Lanegan-led track' separate from the Homme-led majority.

Songs for the Deaf features Homme (most tracks), Oliveri (punk-aggression tracks like Six Shooter), and Lanegan (compression tracks like Medication) as lead vocalists. Vocal democracy as compositional principle expanding band's range.
Grohl restrains technical display in this track — the OPPOSITE of his A Song for the Dead drumming. Restraint serves brevity. Same drummer, different compositional intent within same album.

the_moment_people_remember.primary: Mark Lanegan's whiskey-baritone vocal — the SOUND of his voice is what listeners remember. Distinct identity within QOTSA's vocal range.
the_moment_people_remember.secondary: The 1:55 brevity — listeners notice the track ENDS so quickly that it functions as a statement against rock-album bloat.
the_moment_people_remember.tertiary: The abrupt ending — no fade, no held-note tag, the track stops as decisively as it began
